name: Outpatient Clinic Visit

source.author: TBD — Dr. [Director]
source.derivedFrom: Stanford COACHING Clinic Observation form; History-Taking & Physical-Exam best-practices guides (generalized from pediatric wording).

description: DRAFT for the director to react to — NOT validated. Only criteria judgeable from a spoken transcript are included (technical exam skill, hand-washing, modesty, and preceptor-presentation items were intentionally excluded). Organized by the 5 universal phases.

dimensions.label: Opening the encounter
dimensions.description: Introduction, role, and setting a comfortable tone.

coaching.whyItMatters: A clear, warm opening builds trust and orients the patient before any history is taken.
examplePhrases: Hi, I'm Dr. ___, one of the doctors who'll be seeing you today.
Is it alright if we talk here, or would you prefer somewhere more private?

dimensions.label: Eliciting concerns
dimensions.description: Open-ended exploration of the complaint and the patient's agenda.

coaching.whyItMatters: Starting open and checking for 'what else' surfaces the real reason for the visit and prevents late-breaking concerns.
examplePhrases: Tell me what brought you in today.
Can you tell me more about that?
Is there anything else you'd like to make sure we cover today?

dimensions.label: Showing empathy
dimensions.description: Exploring the patient's perspective and responding to emotion.

coaching.whyItMatters: Understanding fears and expectations — and naming emotion — is what patients remember and what builds the therapeutic relationship.
examplePhrases: What are you most worried this could be?
I can see this is weighing on you.

dimensions.label: Discussing the plan
dimensions.description: Explaining information clearly and agreeing a plan together.

coaching.whyItMatters: Plain-language, shared decision-making improves understanding and adherence.
examplePhrases: Here's what I think is going on, in everyday terms…
What matters most to you as we decide what to do next?

dimensions.label: Closing the encounter
dimensions.description: Checking understanding, safety-netting, and inviting questions.

coaching.whyItMatters: Teach-back and clear safety-netting are the difference between a plan understood and a plan forgotten.
examplePhrases: Just so I know I explained it well — what will you do when you get home?
Come back or call if ___ happens.
What questions do you have for me?

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician introduce themselves and explain their role?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: States name and role and greets the patient.
exemplarQuotes: Hi, I'm Dr. ___, one of the doctors looking after you today.

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician set a comfortable, unrushed tone (e.g. acknowledging privacy/comfort)?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Appears unhurried; attends to the patient's comfort and privacy.

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician begin with open-ended questions and let the patient tell their story?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Starts broad before narrowing; invites the patient to elaborate.
exemplarQuotes: Tell me what brought you in today.
Can you tell me more about that?

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician explore the presenting complaint thoroughly (onset, duration, severity, associated symptoms, what's been tried)?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Characterizes the complaint across its key dimensions.

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician avoid interrupting and give the patient space to speak?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Lets the patient finish; does not cut them off.

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician check for additional concerns ('is there anything else?')?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Explicitly invites further concerns before moving on.
exemplarQuotes: Is there anything else you'd like to cover today?

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician explore the patient's perspective — their ideas, fears, and expectations?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Asks what the patient thinks is going on, what worries them, or what they're hoping for.
exemplarQuotes: What are you most worried this could be?

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician acknowledge and respond to the patient's emotions?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Names or validates the emotion; responds rather than rushing past it.
exemplarQuotes: I can see this is worrying you.

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician convey support, concern, and respect throughout?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Warm, respectful language; conveys that the patient is heard.

criteria.prompt: If a physical exam occurred, did the clinician explain what they were doing and check comfort? (N/A if no exam.)
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Narrates the exam and minimizes surprise/discomfort verbally.
criteria.applicabilityQuestion: Did a physical examination of the patient actually take place in this consultation — for example checking the pulse or blood pressure, listening to the chest, feeling the neck or abdomen, or looking in the eyes or ears?

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician explain things in plain language, avoiding jargon?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Uses everyday words; defines any necessary medical terms.

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician give accurate, appropriate information about the condition and options?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Information given is correct and relevant; avoids false reassurance or overstated certainty.
requiredElements: [Director to specify any must-state facts for common clinic presentations]

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician discuss a clear plan and involve the patient in decisions?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Lays out next steps and invites the patient's preferences.

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician check the patient's understanding (e.g. teach-back)?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Asks the patient to restate the plan in their own words.
exemplarQuotes: Just so I know I explained it well, what will you do when you get home?

criteria.prompt: Did the clinician safety-net (what to watch for, when and how to seek help)?
Did the clinician invite final questions before closing?
criteria.whatGoodLooksLike: Explicitly asks what questions the patient has.
